result.question:
Where can we source data for a 2026 FIFA World Cup content/analytics website that will build a queryable data warehouse? This is for a free, public, no-paywall, SEO-first content site. We need to acquire and republish (derived) data publicly. For EACH data category below, find the best sources and answer: (a) what sources exist (API, bulk dump, or scrape), (b) cost/pricing tiers, (c) licensing & redistribution terms — are we allowed to publicly display/republish derived data, with attribution requirements noted, and flag grey-area sources rather than excluding them (pragmatic licensing), and (d) MOST IMPORTANTLY: the quality/coverage/verifiability gap between FREE sources and PAID/GATED services — i.e. does paying actually get us consistent, verified, complete data that would be difficult or impossible to retrieve/verify from free sources? Be specific about where gated services genuinely win vs where free is just as good.

Data categories needed:
1. Squads, players, coaches (national team rosters for WC 2026) — refreshable
2. Fixtures, results, and live scores for WC 2026
3. Head-to-head history: country vs country all-time match records
4. Player → club → league relationships (which club/league each player plays for) — this is the key "signals" backbone for editorial
5. Team ratings: Elo (eloratings.net) and FIFA world ranking
6. External signals: aggregated prediction-market / betting odds (e.g. odds APIs), and keyword search-volume data (what fans are googling) for content planning

Specifically evaluate and compare these candidate providers where relevant: API-Football (API-Sports), Sportmonks, BALLDONTLIE FIFA World Cup API, Football-Data.org, Wikidata/Wikipedia dumps, Transfermarkt (scraping + terms), FBref/StatsBomb, The Odds API / OddsAPI, Pinnacle/Betfair odds, Google Trends / keyword tools. Conclude with a recommended sourcing stack: which source for each data category, total realistic monthly cost, and an explicit verdict on whether any gated service is worth paying for given free alternatives.
result.summary: For a free, public, SEO-first World Cup 2026 content site that wants to build a queryable warehouse and republish DERIVED data, the cleanest legal foundation is the CC0/public-domain free tier: openfootball/worldcup.json (fixtures, structure, squads, stadiums; CC0, no API key, no attribution) and Wikidata (squads, players, player-to-club/league relationships, coaches; CC0). These cover most editorial backbone categories with full redistribution rights. Paid services (Sportmonks, BALLDONTLIE FIFA API, The Odds API) genuinely win in three narrow places: real-time live scores (sub-15s latency), reliable live match events/lineups, and aggregated betting odds + advanced metrics (xG, predictions)—none of which free CC0 sources deliver consistently or in real time. The most important licensing trap is API-Football (API-Sports): its ToS explicitly grants NO publication license, pushing all redistribution liability onto you, making it a poor fit for a republishing site despite low price. FBref/Opta advanced stats collapsed as a free option in January 2026 (advanced feeds terminated; only basic historical data remains), and Elo (eloratings.net) and search-volume (Google Trends) were not resolved by surviving claims. Recommended stack: openfootball + Wikidata (free, CC0) for squads/fixtures/H2H structure/player-club-league/ratings scaffolding; BALLDONTLIE GOAT ($39.99/mo) OR Sportmonks World Cup Advanced (EUR 69/mo) for live scores/events during the tournament; The Odds API ($30-59/mo) for odds if needed—total realistic spend roughly $40-130/month, concentrated in the tournament window, with most of it avoidable if you can tolerate non-real-time data.

findings.claim: openfootball/worldcup.json is the strongest FREE source for WC 2026 fixtures, schedule structure, knockout rounds, squads, stadiums, groups, and teams, delivered as bulk JSON with no API key, released CC0 (public domain) so derived data can be republished with zero attribution or redistribution restrictions.
findings.evidence: Verified primary: /2026/worldcup.json holds 88 matches (64 group + 24 knockout) with explicit round names through the July 19 2026 Final, plus worldcup.squads.json, worldcup.stadiums.json, worldcup.teams.json, worldcup.groups.json. README: 'No API key required' and 'dedicated to the public domain. Use as you please with no restrictions whatsoever' (CC0-1.0 repo classification). Caveat: community/auto-generated—group fixtures populate after the Dec 2025 draw and live results require manual upstream updates, so it is reliable for structure/schedule but is NOT a live-score feed.

findings.claim: Wikidata is a strong FREE, CC0 source for squads, players, coaches, and crucially the player-to-club-to-league relationships (the editorial 'signals' backbone), with all structured data in main/property/lexeme namespaces released CC0 (public domain), so derived data can be freely republished and redistributed with no mandatory attribution.
findings.evidence: Primary licensing page states verbatim 'All structured data in the main, property and lexeme namespaces is made available under the Creative Commons CC0 License' and 'No attribution is legally required.' CC0 = public domain, no legal barrier to publicly displaying derived data. Caveats: (1) CC0 covers structured data only—text in other namespaces is CC BY-SA 4.0; (2) attribution is a community norm not a legal requirement; (3) CC0 governs Wikidata's copyright position but does not relicense underlying facts imported from sources with their own terms. Note: completeness/freshness of WC-2026-specific rosters on Wikidata was not verified by surviving claims—treat coverage as good-but-verify.

findings.claim: API-Football (API-Sports) is a licensing trap for a republishing content site: its ToS explicitly grants NO license to publish or display the data on websites/apps/products, requires the user to obtain publication permission from rights holders themselves, and disclaims all responsibility for infringing use—so the subscription confers zero redistribution rights.
findings.evidence: ToS verbatim: 'API-Football does not provide a license for the use and publication of the data... Any license or permission to publish the data must be requested by the user from the competent authorities' and 'It is the responsibility of the user to verify and obtain any necessary authorizations or licenses to use or publish data... API-Football declines all responsibility in case of unauthorized or infringing use by the client.' Despite cheap pricing, this is the clearest grey-area/high-risk source for a public republish-derived-data site. Caveat: direct page fetch returns 403 (Cloudflare); confirmation rests on consistent Google-indexed snippets across two queries.

findings.claim: Sportmonks offers the most redistribution-friendly PAID terms among the gated providers: it explicitly allows storage, transfer, and distribution of API data and permits monetizing derived creations without separate approval, prohibiting only direct resale/repackaging of the raw data product without consent.
findings.evidence: ToS verbatim: 'distribution, transfer, and storage of data provided by our services is allowed, but reselling the product is forbidden without our consent' and 'if you use our data to create something based on our data and start earning money from your creation, everything is fine.' Caveat: a separate copyright clause restricts website 'material' to personal/non-commercial use, creating internal tension and NOT cleanly granting the public-display right the project needs—still markedly better than API-Football, but verify the specific public-display use case with Sportmonks.

findings.claim: Sportmonks general Football API pricing runs EUR 29/mo (Starter, 5 leagues) to EUR 99 (Growth, 30), EUR 249 (Pro, 120), and custom Enterprise (2300+ leagues), ~20% off annually, with ALL paid tiers including the same features—squads, coaches, fixtures, live scores, match events, standings, player/team profiles, and advanced statistics (only league count and call capacity differ; odds and >3-season history are paid add-ons).
findings.evidence: Primary pricing page (fetched 2026-06-29) confirms all four tiers and prices and the 20% annual discount, plus 'All plans include the same professional-grade data features. The only difference... is the number of leagues you can select and your API call capacity.' Their March 2026 blog confirms removal of old feature-gating. Self-reported feature list, but appropriate vendor primary source.

findings.claim: Sportmonks sells a dedicated WC 2026 tournament API that genuinely beats free CC0 sources on REAL-TIME data: fixtures, live scores under 15 seconds, match events, lineups, squads, standings, bracket, player stats, covering all 104 matches—at EUR 69/mo (Advanced) for core tournament data or EUR 129/mo (All-In) which adds predictions, real-time xG, pressure index, and pre-match/in-play odds from 50+ bookmakers across 150+ markets.
findings.evidence: Primary product page confirms feature list verbatim, 'Live updates under 15 seconds,' Advanced EUR 69/mo (EUR 55/mo annual) and All-In EUR 129/mo (EUR 103/mo annual) with All-In adding predictions/xG/Pressure Index and odds from 50+ bookmakers/150+ markets. Caveats: latency/uptime figures are vendor-self-reported and not independently benchmarked; minor cross-page inconsistency over whether player stats sit in the EUR 69 tier. This is where paying genuinely wins—real-time scores/events that free CC0 sources cannot deliver.

findings.claim: The BALLDONTLIE FIFA World Cup API is a competitive paid alternative for WC 2018/2022/2026 data—rosters, players, fixtures/results, real-time live scores, group standings, match events, and advanced stats (player ratings, xG, possession)—but its free tier is near-useless (teams/stadiums only); meaningful data requires ALL-STAR ($9.99/mo, +standings) or GOAT ($39.99/mo, all endpoints incl. odds/props/futures/injuries/lineups/stats), with a higher-tier ALL-ACCESS ($299.99/mo) and a 48-hour GOAT trial.
findings.evidence: Primary page confirms three-tournament coverage, full data-type list, real-time scores, and the pricing/feature matrix (Free $0/5rpm teams+stadiums only; ALL-STAR $9.99/60rpm +standings; GOAT $39.99/600rpm all endpoints; ALL-ACCESS $299.99). Caveat: xG/xgot populated only for 2022/2026 (2018 partial). Licensing/redistribution terms for BALLDONTLIE were NOT resolved—a claim asserting 'no explicit licensing terms = grey area' was REFUTED 0-3, so its republication terms remain genuinely unclear and must be checked directly before relying on it for a republishing site.

findings.claim: For aggregated betting/prediction-market odds, The Odds API is a viable paid source: it aggregates many bookmakers (Pinnacle, Betfair, DraftKings, FanDuel, BetMGM, William Hill, 1xBet) across soccer competitions with head-to-head, spreads, totals, and outrights markets; pricing is $30/mo (20K credits) to $249/mo (15M), and—critically—its terms PERMIT commercial use in user-facing websites but PROHIBIT reselling/repackaging/redistributing the data as a standalone data product (own API, feed, or downloadable files).
findings.evidence: Primary site/terms confirm bookmaker and market coverage, the four paid tiers ($30/$59/$119/$249), and terms verbatim: 'We support and encourage the use of our data in websites... including commercial use, provided our data is not the primary product' and 'Do not resell, repackage, or redistribute our data as a standalone data product.' This fits a content site (display/derive odds-based content = OK) but blocks exposing raw odds via your own API. Caveat: soccer/WC coverage leans to 1X2/head-to-head and outrights; spreads/totals are mainly US-sport. NOTE: the previously-claimed free 500-credit tier with 'all sports/all markets' was REFUTED 0-3—do not assume a generous free tier.

findings.claim: FBref/Opta is NO LONGER a reliable FREE source for ADVANCED football stats (xG, progressive passing, shot-creating actions) as of January 2026; only deep historical BASIC data for 100+ competitions remains, and any future advanced data will be at 'dramatically smaller scale.' This eliminates the main free path to advanced metrics and pushes that category toward paid providers (Sportmonks All-In, BALLDONTLIE GOAT).
findings.evidence: Primary Sports Reference blog (Jan 20 2026) verbatim: 'FBref will continue to present the deep historic basic data we have for over one hundred competitions, but if we present advanced data in the future it will certainly have a dramatically smaller scale.' Corroborated by multiple third parties reporting the advanced provider terminated feeds and required deletion in Jan 2026. Caveats: claims that advanced data was 'permanently deleted/unretrievable' (1-2) and that the provider was specifically StatsBomb (0-3) were NOT confirmed—the provider identity and permanence are uncertain, but basic-data persistence and advanced-data loss are confirmed.

result.caveats: Time-sensitivity: all pricing was verified 2026-06-29 and odds/sports-API pricing changes frequently; re-check before committing. Tournament-specific tiers (Sportmonks WC API, BALLDONTLIE) are seasonal and most cost is concentrated in the June-July 2026 window, so you can subscribe only during the tournament. Vendor self-reporting: live-score latency ('under 15s'), uptime ('99.99%'), and bookmaker/market counts are marketing figures not independently benchmarked. Licensing grey areas remain: BALLDONTLIE's redistribution terms are genuinely unresolved (a 'no explicit terms = grey area' claim was refuted, but no positive grant was confirmed either)—verify directly. Sportmonks has internal ToS tension between its permissive data-distribution clause and a restrictive website-material copyright clause; it does not cleanly grant the public-display right the project needs. The openfootball and Wikidata CC0 grants are solid, but their WC-2026 COMPLETENESS and FRESHNESS (post-draw fixtures, live results, current player-club assignments) were not quantitatively verified by surviving claims—both are 'reliable for structure, verify for live/current.' Free CC0 sources do not provide real-time live scores or advanced metrics; that is the one place paying is clearly justified.
openQuestions: Elo ratings (eloratings.net) and FIFA world ranking: no surviving claim addressed their availability, licensing, or scrape-ability—how are these to be sourced and may derived ratings be republished? eloratings.net has no formal license/API and is a likely scrape grey-area.

Keyword/search-volume data (Google Trends and keyword tools) for content planning: completely unaddressed by surviving claims—what are the cost/licensing terms and is Google Trends data republishable?

Head-to-head country-vs-country all-time records: no source was confirmed as authoritative/complete for full historical H2H; can openfootball historical repos + Wikidata be combined to reconstruct verifiable all-time records, or is a paid provider needed?

What are BALLDONTLIE's actual redistribution/republication terms (a positive grant was never confirmed), and does Transfermarkt offer any usable terms for player-club-league data given its scraping restrictions (Transfermarkt was named as a candidate but produced no surviving claim)?

refuted.claim: The Odds API offers a free Starter tier of 500 credits/month with access to all sports, most bookmakers, all betting markets, and historical odds.
Sportmonks permits commercial use of its data (betting, fantasy, gaming) but places regulatory/licensing compliance on the customer; the page gives no explicit republishing/redistribution terms.

The API documentation contains no explicit licensing terms, redistribution permissions, attribution requirements, or data-reuse policy, leaving republication of derived data legally unclear (grey area).

As of mid-January 2026, FBref's advanced soccer data provider (StatsBomb) terminated Sports Reference's access to the data feeds and required immediate deletion, so FBref no longer hosts advanced soccer stats (xG, etc.).
The advanced data has been permanently deleted from FBref/Stathead and cannot be requested or retrieved, eliminating it as a sourcing option going forward.
